13.text: Standard math for attention scaling and log/exp.

15.text: Random for weight init, sampling, and shuffling.

22.text: Build the dataset path relative to this script.

29.text: Load all names, one per line.

43.text: Single layer — keeps training fast for the paging demo.

49.text: Same Value autograd engine from Lab 01 — exact copy. Each node is a scalar that tracks its computation graph for automatic differentiation.

50.text: __slots__ for memory efficiency — especially matters here because we'll have thousands of Value nodes during training.

52.text: Constructor: scalar data, parent nodes, and local derivatives.

55.text: Child nodes in the computation graph.

60.text: New node: sum of data, both local grads are 1.

64.text: Product rule: d(a*b)/da = b, d(a*b)/db = a.

69.text: ReLU: gradient is 1 if positive, 0 if negative.

90.text: Chain rule: multiply local grad by parent grad.

94.text: Weight matrix factory: creates a 2D list of small random Value nodes.

99.text: Token embeddings: map token IDs to vectors.

100.text: Position embeddings: map positions to vectors.

101.text: Separate output head (no weight tying in this lab).

103.text: Create attention weights for each layer.

104.text: Compact loop over the four attention projections.

108.text: Flatten all parameters into a single list for the optimizer.

112.text: Matrix-vector multiply: dot product of each weight row with input.

113.text: Returns a vector of dot products.

116.text: Dual-mode softmax: works with both Value nodes (training) and plain floats (inference).

117.text: Type dispatch: check if we're in autograd mode.

118.text: Max subtraction for numerical stability (Value mode).

128.text: Compute scale factor in one expression.

132.text: Dual-mode ReLU: dispatches to Value.relu() or plain max.

133.text: Check if we're in autograd mode.

134.text: Value mode: use the tracked relu for gradient computation.

135.text: Plain float mode: simple max(0, x).

143.text: Contiguous KV cache: the traditional approach. Pre-allocates max_seq_len slots per layer upfront, regardless of actual sequence length. Simple but wasteful — like malloc'ing the maximum possible size for every request.

146.text: Constructor: specify max length, layers, and dimension.

150.text: Pre-allocate full K cache: n_layers × max_seq_len × dims. All slots allocated even if most stay empty.

151.text: Pre-allocate full V cache: same shape, same waste.

152.text: Track how many positions are actually filled.

153.text: Total allocated slots — this number stays fixed regardless of actual usage, which is the waste PagedAttention solves.

156.text: Reset for a new sequence — just moves the length counter back to zero.

157.text: "Free" memory by resetting the pointer. The pre-allocated arrays stay in memory.

159.text: Append a new K,V pair to the cache at the current position.

160.text: Write K vector to the next open slot.

161.text: Write V vector to the same slot.

162.text: Only increment length after the last layer writes, since all layers share the same position counter.

165.text: Retrieve all cached K,V pairs up to the current position.

166.text: Return slices — only the filled portion, not the empty pre-allocated tail.

170.text: Ratio of used to allocated. For short sequences in a large buffer, this is often just 10-20%.

173.text: Block size for paged allocation: 4 tokens per block. Like a page size in virtual memory — the fundamental unit of allocation.

176.text: PagedKVCache: the core innovation behind vLLM. Instead of pre-allocating max_seq_len per sequence, allocate small fixed-size blocks on demand. A block table maps logical positions to physical block IDs — exactly like OS page tables map virtual addresses to physical frames.

183.text: Constructor: takes total physical blocks available, layers, dimensions, and block size.

184.text: Store the block size (tokens per block).

187.text: Total physical blocks in the pool.

188.text: Physical K storage: one block = block_size_tokens × dims. Blocks are the atomic allocation unit.

189.text: Physical V storage: same shape as K blocks.

190.text: Free block list — like the OS free frame list. Blocks are allocated from here on demand and returned when sequences finish.

191.text: Block tables: maps (seq_id, layer) → list of physical block IDs. This IS the page table.

196.text: Allocate one physical block from the free list.

197.text: Out of blocks = out of memory. In production vLLM, this triggers request preemption.

198.text: Raise an error if the pool is exhausted.

199.text: Pop from the free list and return the physical block ID.

201.text: Initialize a new sequence's page table — one empty block list per layer.

202.text: Create the page table: each layer gets its own list of physical block mappings.

205.text: Append K,V to the paged cache — the hot path that makes paging work.

206.text: Current sequence length determines which logical block and slot.

207.text: Which logical block this position falls in (integer division).

209.text: If this logical block doesn't have a physical block yet, allocate one. This is the demand paging — blocks are allocated only when first touched, not upfront.

210.text: Allocate a physical block from the free pool.

211.text: Map: logical block → physical block in the page table.

215.text: Look up the physical block ID for this logical block.

216.text: Write K vector into the physical block at the right slot.

217.text: Write V vector into the same slot.

218.text: Increment length only after the last layer.

221.text: Reconstruct the full K,V history by walking the page table — translating logical positions to physical locations.

225.text: Logical block index for this position.

227.text: Translate logical → physical via the page table. This indirection is the price of paging, but it's O(1).

228.text: Read K vector from the physical block.

229.text: Read V vector from the physical block.

232.text: Free a sequence's blocks back to the pool — like deallocating pages in an OS.

233.text: Return all physical blocks from every layer.

234.text: Extend the free list — blocks are immediately available for other sequences.

240.text: Ratio: with paging, this tracks actual usage closely. No wasted pre-allocation.

242.text: Prefix sharing: the killer feature of paged caches. Multiple sequences share the same physical blocks for a common prefix — zero-copy, just duplicate the page table entries.

244.text: Create a new sequence for the destination.

245.text: Ceiling division: how many blocks the shared prefix spans.

248.text: For each block in the prefix...

249.text: Get the source's physical block ID.

250.text: Point the destination's page table at the SAME physical block. No data copy — both sequences read from identical memory.

252.text: Set the destination's length to the prefix length.

253.text: Return how many blocks were shared.

261.text: Unified GPT forward pass: processes one token at a time using any cache implementation. The cache abstraction means the model code doesn't know or care whether it's paged or contiguous.

262.text: Default to the training state dict if no weights provided.

264.text: Embed: look up token and position vectors, add them.

269.text: Compute Q, K, V projections in one compact expression using a generator.

270.text: Store K,V in the cache — the cache handles the allocation strategy transparently.

271.text: Retrieve all historical K,V pairs — the cache translates logical to physical positions if paged.

278.text: Scaled dot-product attention against all cached keys.

287.text: ReLU activation (dispatches to correct mode via relu()).

305.text: Create a contiguous cache for this training step. Training always uses contiguous — paging is an inference optimization.

306.text: Initialize the cache for a new sequence.

309.text: Input and target for this position.

310.text: Forward pass through the model with the cache.

312.text: Cross-entropy loss: negative log probability of the correct next token.

329.text: Strip autograd wrappers: extract plain floats for fast inference.

332.text: Generate a name using any cache implementation. The cache parameter abstracts the allocation strategy completely.

333.text: Initialize the cache for a new sequence.

334.text: Start with BOS, empty sample buffer.

336.text: Forward pass using plain-float weights and the provided cache.

348.text: Section header for the comparison experiment.

350.text: Create a contiguous cache with max-size pre-allocation.

355.text: Print the generated name and token count.

356.text: Show allocated vs. used slots — the waste is visible here.

358.text: 32 physical blocks for the paged cache pool.

359.text: Create paged cache with the same capacity.

360.text: Same seed ensures identical random choices.

364.text: Show blocks allocated on demand — much less waste than contiguous.

365.text: Assert outputs match — the cache strategy must not affect model output, only memory efficiency.

371.text: Section header for prefix sharing demo.

373.text: Create a paged cache with plenty of blocks for the prefix sharing demo.

374.text: Tokenize a short prefix "an" — simulates a shared system prompt.

375.text: Initialize the prefix sequence in the cache.

376.text: Run each prefix token through the model to populate the KV cache.

377.text: Forward pass stores K,V in the cache at each position.

379.text: Check how many tokens the prefix cached.

380.text: Count how many blocks the prefix occupies.

383.text: Create 3 sequences that share this prefix.

385.text: Share the prefix's physical blocks with the new sequence — zero data copying, just page table duplication.

386.text: Report how many blocks were shared (should match the prefix's block count).

387.text: Show that sharing adds zero new block allocations.

388.text: Compare: without sharing, we'd need N copies of the prefix blocks.

400.text: Large paged cache pool for generating many names.

402.text: Unique seed per sample for variety.

403.text: Generate using the paged cache, each sample gets its own sequence ID.

406.text: Free the sequence's blocks back to the pool — memory is recycled for the next sample.
